content:
Hi all,

I was just wondering if there are terms such as Aliemus or Gnosis that you would like to keep the same across all languages or at least the ones that comes from latin.
Are terms such as warden, chorus and assault prefered over clasic RPG terms offensive, defensive and support?

Also. I would like to know if the term awakener is intended, since my understanding of It is that they are awakened by the keeper hence they should be awakened. This last point may be my lack of knowledge about the lore.

No clue where else to post this, but I want to be helpful, so I'd like to share some knowledge that I find useful!
I figured I'd make a guide on this first to train my guide-making skills before something more important, and if this helps anyone here at all then it'll have been worth it.
I'm not fluent in Excel per se so there totally could be a way better way to do this, but this is how I keep track of what lines I've edited so I can filter them later!
The first thing I do is separate out what I'm working with into a different worksheet, make sure I bring the header row along, and cull any language columns I'm not working with.

Next, I duplicate the column I intend to be working within with a quick copy-paste, and make the whole thing a table. You can quickly select everything you're working with by selecting a cell within that chunk and using Ctrl+A.
Now that I have **EN** (column I:I) and **EN2** (column J:J), I hide I:I because I want it to stay as-is, and I work entirely within J:J.

Here's the fun part:
In **Home** -> **Styles** -> **Conditional Formatting**, click **Manage Rules**.
Make sure it's set to Show formatting rules for: **This Table**, and click **New Rule**.
Click **"Use a formula to determine which cells to format"**, and input the following:
`=NOT(EXACT($I1,$J1))`
-# where "`I`" is the column you hid, and "`J`" is the column you're working in.
In **Format**, go to **Fill** and pick your preferred color. I like this pleasantly unoffensive light green.
Click **OK**, and in **Applies to**, input `=$A:$J` and click **OK**.

At this point, Excel really likes to shit its absolute fucking pants for no reason whatsoever, so if it does that to you, go back to **Conditional Formatting** -> **Manage Rules**, select the stupid idiot rule, and click **Edit Rule**.
Put `=NOT(EXACT($I1,$J1))` back in, and reassure Excel that it's going to be **OK**, **OK**.

Now, whenever you edit a cell in column J:J, the entire row will be green so you can find what you have and haven't changed later on! <:blushcat:971080649769353267>
(this will be potentially relevant in the next guide I make)
